narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right in line with the typical student's 3.21—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic records, family background, and other details like your LSAT score of 32 and plans to attend full-time. The model estimated a 40.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to pass with high confidence to maintain strong outcomes. The key driver pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lowest group compared to peers, strongly influencing the overall risk assessment. Counterbalancing this somewhat was your family income in the lower-middle bracket, which the model viewed favorably as it has historically correlated with bar exam success for students like you, pulling the prediction away from failure. Your undergraduate GPA also helped in this way, as its alignment with typical strong performers nudged the odds toward passing despite other concerns. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students mainly due to that low Year 3 decile and a below-average LSAT of 32 versus the usual 36.7, which together overshadowed your full-time commitment and average undergrad record. While your circumstances as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a modest background show determination, the model weighed recent school performance as the most critical factor here. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, and focus on strengthening your final-year grades to improve your decile ranking—perhaps through tutoring or study groups. Building a stronger narrative in your application about overcoming your Year 3 dip could also help appeal to admissions reviewers. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these exact areas before reapplying. With targeted improvements, your full-time dedication and solid foundation position you well for another shot at admission.
